title: Attention Is All You Need
text: The dominant sequence transduction models are based on complex recurrent or convolutional neural networks that include an encoder and a decoder. The best performing models also connect the encoder and decoder through an attention mechanism. We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms.
author: Ashish Vaswani

title: BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers
text: We introduce a new language representation model called BERT, which stands for Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers. Unlike recent language representation models, BERT is designed to pre-train deep bidirectional representations from unlabeled text by jointly conditioning on both left and right context in all layers.
author: Jacob Devlin

title: Language Models are Few-Shot Learners
text: Recent work has demonstrated substantial gains on many NLP tasks and benchmarks by pre-training on a large corpus of text followed by fine-tuning on a specific task. While typically task-agnostic in architecture, this method still requires task-specific fine-tuning datasets of thousands or tens of thousands of examples. By contrast, humans can generally perform a new language task from only a few examples.
author: Tom B. Brown

title: Mastering the game of Go with deep neural networks
text: The game of Go has long been viewed as the most challenging of classic games for artificial intelligence owing to its enormous search space and the difficulty of evaluating board positions and moves. Here we introduce a new approach to computer Go that uses value networks to evaluate board positions and policy networks to select moves.
author: David Silver

title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
text: Deeper neural networks are more difficult to train. We present a residual learning framework to ease the training of networks that are substantially deeper than those used previously. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learning residual functions with reference to the layer inputs.
author: Kaiming He

title: Generative Adversarial Nets
text: We propose a new framework for estimating generative models via an adversarial process, in which we simultaneously train two models: a generative model G that captures the data distribution, and a discriminative model D that estimates the probability that a sample came from the training data rather than G.
author: Ian Goodfellow

title: Playing Atari with Deep Reinforcement Learning
text: We present the first deep learning model to successfully learn control policies directly from high-dimensional sensory input using reinforcement learning. The model is a convolutional neural network, trained with a variant of Q-learning, whose input is raw pixels and whose output is a value function estimating future rewards.
author: Volodymyr Mnih

title: Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization
text: We introduce Adam, an algorithm for first-order gradient-based optimization of stochastic objective functions, based on adaptive estimates of lower-order moments. The method is straightforward to implement, is computationally efficient, has little memory requirements, and is well suited for highly non-stationary problems.
author: Diederik P. Kingma

title: YOLO9000: Better, Faster, Stronger
text: We introduce YOLO9000, a state-of-the-art, real-time object detection system that can detect over 9000 object categories. First we propose improvements to the YOLO detection method, both novel and drawn from prior work, which greatly improves its accuracy and speed.
author: Joseph Redmon

title: Distributed Representations of Words and Phrases and their Compositionality
text: The recently introduced continuous Skip-gram model is an efficient method for learning high-quality distributed vector representations that capture a large number of precise syntactic and semantic word relationships. In this paper we present several extensions that improve both the quality of the vectors and the training speed.
author: Tomas Mikolov

title: Evaluating Large Language Models Trained on Code
text: We introduce Codex, a GPT language model fine-tuned on publicly available code from GitHub, and study its Python code-writing capabilities. A distinct production version of Codex powers GitHub Copilot.
author: Mark Chen

title: Training language models to follow instructions
text: Making language models bigger does not inherently make them better at following a user's intent. For example, large language models can generate outputs that are untruthful, toxic, or simply not helpful. In this paper, we show an avenue for aligning language models with user intent on a wide range of tasks.
author: Long Ouyang

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
text: Large pre-trained language models have been shown to store factual knowledge in their parameters, and achieve state-of-the-art results when fine-tuned on downstream NLP tasks. However, their ability to access and precisely manipulate knowledge is still limited, hence we introduce RAG models which combine pre-trained parametric and non-parametric memory for language generation.
author: Patrick Lewis

title: A Survey of Vector Database Systems
text: The advent of deep learning has led to the emergence of vector database systems, which manage high-dimensional embeddings representing unstructured data. These massive dense vectors necessitate highly efficient indexing and query mechanisms such as Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) search using HNSW or IVF architectures.
author: Hanwen Liu
